seasons.n: Sweeter than turnips, especially after frost. Excellent mashed, roasted, or added to stews.

seasons.st: Don't wash until eating. Refrigerate on paper towels. Use within 2-3 days.

seasons.n: CA grows 90% of US strawberries. Available almost year-round but spring berries are a different universe of flavor.
seasons.st: Don't wash until eating. Refrigerate on paper towels. Use within 2-3 days.

seasons.n: Nutty, slightly sweet, with a crisp water chestnut texture when raw. Roast until caramelized for the best experience.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Garnet and Jewel varieties dominate CA. True yams are a different plant entirely — what Americans call yams are sweet potatoes.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dark place — never refrigerate raw (damages texture). Keeps several weeks.

seasons.n: The papery husk peels off to reveal a sticky, tart fruit essential for salsa verde. Smaller ones tend to be sweeter.
seasons.st: Leave husks on. Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: The papery husk peels off to reveal a sticky, tart fruit essential for salsa verde. Smaller ones tend to be sweeter.
seasons.st: Leave husks on. Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Peak CA heirlooms in August are one of the great food experiences. Never refrigerate a tomato — it kills flavor and texture.
seasons.st: Always room temp, stem-end down. Eat within a few days of ripening.

seasons.n: Peak CA heirlooms in August are one of the great food experiences. Never refrigerate a tomato — it kills flavor and texture.
seasons.st: Always room temp, stem-end down. Eat within a few days of ripening.

seasons.n: Young turnips are mild and sweet. Hakurei (Japanese) turnips are small, white, and can be eaten raw like radishes.
seasons.st: Remove greens. Refrigerate roots in a bag for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Look for a pronounced yellow ground spot and hollow sound when tapped. Seedless varieties dominate but seeded have more flavor. Imperial Valley starts May.
seasons.st: Whole: room temp. Cut: refrigerate and eat within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Look for a pronounced yellow ground spot and hollow sound when tapped. Seedless varieties dominate but seeded have more flavor. Imperial Valley starts May.
seasons.st: Whole: room temp. Cut: refrigerate and eat within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Vibrant magenta flesh, watermelon-bubblegum flavor. Handle with care — tiny invisible glochids are devilish.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Use tongs or thick gloves when handling.

seasons.n: Vibrant magenta flesh, watermelon-bubblegum flavor. Handle with care — tiny invisible glochids are devilish.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Use tongs or thick gloves when handling.

seasons.n: Minneola tangelos have a distinctive knob at the stem. Very juicy, tangy-sweet — a grapefruit-tangerine cross.
seasons.st: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Tastes like a mild, sweet broccoli stem. Excellent raw, shredded into slaws, or roasted. Peel the tough outer skin.
seasons.st: Remove leaves. Refrigerate for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Nature's fractal — a psychedelic relative of cauliflower. Nuttier, more delicate flavor. Stunning in presentation.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Fresh olives are inedible raw — they must be cured. CA olive oil harvest peaks in fall. Mission and Manzanilla are key CA varieties.
seasons.st: Process immediately if curing. Olive oil: store in dark, cool place.

seasons.n: Fresh olives are inedible raw — they must be cured. CA olive oil harvest peaks in fall. Mission and Manzanilla are key CA varieties.
seasons.st: Process immediately if curing. Olive oil: store in dark, cool place.

seasons.n: Flavor King (mid-Aug) is widely considered THE best pluot — sweet, spicy, intensely complex. Dapple Dandy/Dinosaur Egg (mid-Aug to mid-Sep). Flavor Queen (early Jul) is honey-sweet. Pluots typically 2-5 more brix than plums. Created by Zaiger Genetics in Modesto, CA.
seasons.st: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe — use within 3 days.

seasons.n: Ugly but delicious — intense celery flavor. Classic in French rémoulade or pureed into soup.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Not actually broccoli — more related to turnips. Pleasantly bitter. Quick blanch before sautéing tames the bite.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a damp towel. Use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Often unfairly maligned — a good honeydew is floral and honey-sweet. Should feel slightly waxy, not rock-hard.
seasons.st: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe. Cut melon keeps 2-3 days.

seasons.n: Often unfairly maligned — a good honeydew is floral and honey-sweet. Should feel slightly waxy, not rock-hard.
seasons.st: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe. Cut melon keeps 2-3 days.

seasons.n: CA produces 80% of the world's almonds. Fresh green almonds in spring are a brief farmers market delicacy — tart and crunchy.
seasons.st: Fresh almonds: refrigerate, use within days. Dried: cool, dark place for months.

seasons.n: Fresh-harvest walnuts in fall are milder and less bitter than stored. CA grows 99% of US walnuts.
seasons.st: Shell and refrigerate or freeze. In-shell keeps months in a cool place.

seasons.n: Fresh-harvest walnuts in fall are milder and less bitter than stored. CA grows 99% of US walnuts.
seasons.st: Shell and refrigerate or freeze. In-shell keeps months in a cool place.

seasons.n: Young garlic harvested before the bulb forms. Mild, green-onion-like with a gentle garlic warmth. A spring farmers market treasure.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Young garlic harvested before the bulb forms. Mild, green-onion-like with a gentle garlic warmth. A spring farmers market treasure.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Tangy, green-bean-like flavor. De-spine, slice, and grill or sauté until the slime cooks off. Essential in Mexican cuisine.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Tangy, green-bean-like flavor. De-spine, slice, and grill or sauté until the slime cooks off. Essential in Mexican cuisine.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Babcock (late Jun) is the classic. Snow series and Arctic whites at farmers markets. Sweeter, more floral than yellow. More delicate — buy at farmers markets.
seasons.st: Ripen at room temp, then refrigerate. Eat within 2-3 days of ripening.

seasons.n: Galaxy is the main variety. Flat shape, melting texture, very sweet. Increasingly available commercially.
seasons.st: Ripen at room temp, then refrigerate. Eat within 2-3 days of ripening.

seasons.n: Sugar converts to starch rapidly after picking. Same-day corn from a farmers market is a completely different food.
seasons.st: Eat the day you buy it. If you must store, keep in the husk in the fridge — use within 2 days.

seasons.n: Spring crop is sweeter. Eat pod and all — the snap is the whole point. Best raw or barely cooked.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Fall crop, slightly less sweet than spring but still excellent.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Flat pods, mild sweet flavor. Essential in stir-fries. De-string before cooking.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Year-round in CA, peak spring. Both white and green parts are usable. Grilled whole scallions are a revelation.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within a week.

seasons.n: The all-purpose cooking onion. Spring harvest is sweeter and juicier, fall storage onions are more pungent.
seasons.st: Store whole in a cool, dry, dark place. Cut onions: wrap tightly and fridge for a few days.

seasons.n: Imperial Valley's warm climate produces early-season onions.
seasons.st: Store whole in a cool, dry, dark place. Cut onions: wrap tightly and fridge for a few days.

seasons.n: Milder than yellow. Best raw in salads and sandwiches. Quick-pickle for tacos.
seasons.st: Store whole in a cool, dry, dark place. Cut onions: wrap tightly and fridge for a few days.

seasons.n: More complex than onion — sweet, slightly garlicky. Essential in vinaigrettes and French cooking.
seasons.st: Store like onions in a cool, dry, dark place. Keeps for months.

seasons.n: Year-round in CA, best in cool months. The go-to for kimchi, dumplings, and Asian soups.
seasons.st: Refrigerate wrapped. Keeps 1-2 weeks.

seasons.n: Cool-season crop. Mild, juicy. Grate for condiment, braise, or pickle. Watermelon radishes have stunning interiors.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Classic fall squash. Dark green skin, orange flesh. Roast halved with butter and brown sugar.
seasons.st: Whole squash keeps for months in a cool dry place. Cut: refrigerate and use within a week.

seasons.n: Classic fall squash. Dark green skin, orange flesh. Flavor improves with a few weeks of curing after harvest.
seasons.st: Whole squash keeps for months in a cool dry place. Cut: refrigerate and use within a week.

seasons.n: The edible-skin squash. Cream-colored with green stripes. Sweet, creamy, no peeling needed.
seasons.st: Whole squash keeps for months in a cool dry place. Cut: refrigerate and use within a week.

seasons.n: Japanese pumpkin. Dense, dry, intensely sweet flesh — like chestnut. Excellent roasted or in soup.
seasons.st: Whole squash keeps for months in a cool dry place. Cut: refrigerate and use within a week.

seasons.n: Smaller pods (under 3 inches) are more tender. High heat cooking (grilling, roasting, frying) reduces sliminess.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag. Use within 3 days — deteriorates quickly.

seasons.n: Sungold is the standout — bursting sweet-tart flavor. Sweeter and more reliable than full-size tomatoes.
seasons.st: Room temp always. Never refrigerate. Use within a few days.

seasons.n: SoCal coastal climate extends the season. Dry-farmed cherry tomatoes have concentrated flavor.
seasons.st: Room temp always. Never refrigerate. Use within a few days.

seasons.n: Slender, thin-skinned, fewer seeds than globe. Cooks faster, more tender. Excellent grilled whole.
seasons.st: Room temp if using within 2 days. Refrigerate loosely wrapped for up to a week.

seasons.n: Fresh limas are nothing like frozen — buttery, sweet, nutty. Christmas limas have stunning maroon markings.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in pods. Shell just before cooking. Use within a few days.

seasons.n: Genovese is the standard for pesto. Thai basil holds up to heat better. Pinch flowers to extend harvest.
seasons.st: Do not refrigerate — cold blackens leaves. Store stems in water on the counter.

seasons.n: Genovese is the standard for pesto. Thai basil holds up to heat better. Pinch flowers to extend harvest.
seasons.st: Do not refrigerate — cold blackens leaves. Store stems in water on the counter.

seasons.n: Year-round in CA. Cool-season herb — bolts quickly in heat. Fall crop is also excellent. Include stems in cooking — more flavor than leaves.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within 5-7 days.

seasons.n: Flat-leaf (Italian) has more flavor. Curly is mainly for garnish. Much more than decoration — a key ingredient in chimichurri, tabbouleh, gremolata.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Keeps about a week.

seasons.n: Truly year-round evergreen perennial. Grows like a weed on the CA coast. Woody stems make excellent skewers for grilling.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate for 2+ weeks. Dries easily.

seasons.n: Year-round in CA, most aromatic in warm months. Lemon thyme is a distinct variety with citrus notes.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Keeps about 2 weeks.

seasons.n: Spearmint is milder, peppermint is more intense. Grows aggressively — if you plant it, contain it.
seasons.st: Store stems in water on the counter or wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate.

seasons.n: Fern-like fronds with anise-caraway flavor. Essential for pickles, salmon, potato salad. Flowers are edible too.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within a week — wilts fast.

seasons.n: Year-round in CA. Mexican oregano is a different plant with a more robust flavor. Dried oregano is often more potent than fresh.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Dries very well.

seasons.n: Velvety leaves, earthy-peppery flavor. Essential for brown butter sage pasta and Thanksgiving stuffing.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Cultivated year-round on supplemented sawdust logs. Rich umami flavor. Remove tough stems before cooking.
seasons.st: Store in a paper bag in the fridge. Never seal in plastic — they get slimy. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Fast-growing cultivated variety. Delicate, velvety texture. Excellent seared at high heat until edges crisp.
seasons.st: Store in a paper bag in the fridge. Very perishable — use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Baby portobello. Cultivated year-round. More flavor than white button mushrooms. Versatile all-purpose mushroom.
seasons.st: Store in a paper bag in the fridge. Never seal in plastic — they get slimy.

seasons.n: Mature cremini. Cultivated year-round. Meaty texture makes it a popular burger substitute. Scrape out gills to reduce dark liquid.
seasons.st: Store in a paper bag in the fridge. Use within 5-7 days.

seasons.n: Hotter than jalapeño, bright flavor. The workhorse chile for fresh salsas. Summer heat produces spicier peppers.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 2 weeks. Freeze whole for long-term storage.

seasons.n: SoCal extends the pepper season. Smaller serranos tend to be hotter.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 2 weeks. Freeze whole for long-term storage.

seasons.n: Fruity, floral heat — far more complex than just hot. Orange variety is most common. Scotch bonnet is a close relative.
seasons.st: Handle with gloves. Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 2 weeks. Freeze for months.

seasons.n: Mild, rich, and essential for chiles rellenos. When dried, it becomes ancho. Roast and peel for best flavor.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 2 weeks.

seasons.n: Mild, versatile chile named after Anaheim, CA. Excellent roasted. Very similar to New Mexico/Hatch chiles.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 2 weeks. Roast and freeze for year-round use.

seasons.n: Small but extremely hot. Essential in Thai and Southeast Asian cooking. Both red and green are used.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Dries and freezes well.

seasons.n: Long, dark green chile. Mild to medium heat. When dried, becomes the pasilla. Used in mole sauces.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag. Use within a week.

seasons.n: Always imported. Cook at all stages: green (starchy, like potato), yellow (sweet), black (very sweet for maduros).
seasons.st: Room temp to ripen. Green plantains keep for weeks. Ripe ones refrigerate for a few days.

seasons.n: Always imported. No meaningful seasonal quality variation.
seasons.st: Room temp to ripen. Green plantains keep for weeks. Ripe ones refrigerate for a few days.

seasons.n: Always imported. No meaningful seasonal quality variation.
seasons.st: Room temp to ripen. Green plantains keep for weeks. Ripe ones refrigerate for a few days.

seasons.n: Always imported. Young coconuts (white husk) have more water and soft jelly flesh. Brown coconuts have firmer meat.
seasons.st: Whole coconuts keep for months. Once cracked

seasons.n: Always imported. No meaningful seasonal quality variation.
seasons.st: Whole coconuts keep for months. Once cracked

seasons.n: Always imported. Look for firm, smooth skin — wrinkles mean old. Young ginger (pink tips) is milder and appears briefly at Asian markets.
seasons.st: Unpeeled in the fridge for weeks. Freeze whole — grate directly from frozen.

seasons.n: Always imported. No meaningful seasonal quality variation.
seasons.st: Unpeeled in the fridge for weeks. Freeze whole — grate directly from frozen.

seasons.n: Developed in Fresno CA in 1952. Harvested green or left to ripen red for more heat and sweetness.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwrapped in crisper drawer for up to a week. Freeze whole for longer storage.

seasons.n: Japanese variety popular at farmers markets. About one in ten is unexpectedly spicy. Ready to pick just 60 days after transplant.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to a week. Best eaten within a few days of harvest.

seasons.n: Spanish tapas pepper similar to shishito. Pick small for mild flavor -- larger ones pack more heat.
seasons.st: Refrigerate loosely wrapped for up to a week. Blister in hot oil with coarse salt.

seasons.n: Named for Hatch NM but grown in CA. Short season drives a cult following. Roast over open flame to blister and peel.
seasons.st: Roast and freeze in batches for year-round use. Fresh chiles keep 1-2 weeks refrigerated.

seasons.n: Long thin hot pepper widely grown in the Central Valley. Typically dried and ground into cayenne powder.
seasons.st: Dry in a dehydrator or string into ristras. Fresh peppers keep 1-2 weeks refrigerated.

seasons.n: Mild sweet pepper often pickled or used in sandwiches. Named for its curved yellow shape.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 2 weeks. Excellent pickled in vinegar brine.

seasons.n: Medium-hot pepper similar to banana pepper but with more bite. Turns orange then red as it matures.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Good for pickling or stuffing.

seasons.n: Snack-size sweet peppers in red yellow and orange. Greenhouse production extends the season.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a produce bag for up to 2 weeks. Eat raw or roast whole.

seasons.n: Mild Italian frying pepper with thin walls. Popular in Latin and Italian cooking.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Saute in olive oil until blistered.

seasons.n: Fiery Caribbean pepper closely related to habanero. Most US supply imported from Mexico and Caribbean.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Freeze whole for longer storage. Handle with gloves.

seasons.n: Harvest male flowers in the morning when fully open. Fragile and best used the same day.
seasons.st: Refrigerate on a damp paper towel in a single layer. Use within 1-2 days.

seasons.n: Peppery edible flower and leaves. Blooms prolifically in coastal California. Heat stress can make flavor too pungent.
seasons.st: Keep stems in a glass of water like cut flowers. Petals wilt quickly -- use same day.

seasons.n: English lavender varieties are best for culinary use. Grown commercially on the Central Coast. Very drought tolerant.
seasons.st: Dry by hanging bundles upside down. Store dried buds in an airtight jar away from light.

seasons.n: Cool-season flower that blooms spring and fall in California. Petals add golden color to rice and soups.
seasons.st: Refrigerate petals in a damp paper towel for a few days. Dry petals for tea or seasoning.

seasons.n: German chamomile grown as an annual in California. Harvest flower heads when petals fold back from the center.
seasons.st: Dry flowers on a screen in a warm airy spot. Store dried in an airtight container for tea.

seasons.n: Mild onion-flavored purple flowers that appear in spring. Pull apart into individual florets for garnish.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a produce bag for 2-3 days. Separate florets and scatter over salads or eggs.

seasons.n: Star-shaped blue flowers with a cucumber-like flavor. Self-seeds readily in California gardens.
seasons.st: Use flowers immediately -- they wilt within hours. Freeze in ice cubes for drinks.

seasons.n: Gem and signet marigold varieties are edible with a citrusy tang. Common in Mexican and South Asian cooking.
seasons.st: Refrigerate petals in a sealed container for 2-3 days. Dry for longer storage.

seasons.n: Major CA citrus crop from the San Joaquin Valley. Varieties include Clementine Murcott and Satsuma.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Room temperature is fine for a few days.

seasons.n: Wet-harvested in Oregon bogs near Bandon. Not grown commercially in California.
seasons.st: Refrigerate fresh berries for up to 2 months. Freeze for up to a year.

seasons.n: About 300 acres of quince grow in the San Joaquin Valley south of Fresno. Must be cooked -- too hard and astringent raw.
seasons.st: Store at room temperature until fragrant then refrigerate. Keeps 2-3 weeks in the fridge.

seasons.n: Cross between raspberry loganberry and blackberry developed in Anaheim in the 1920s. Knott's Berry Farm made it famous.
seasons.st: Highly perishable. Refrigerate in a single layer and eat within 2-3 days. Freezes well.

seasons.n: Common backyard tree throughout the Central Valley. White varieties ripen first followed by red and black.
seasons.st: Extremely perishable -- eat or freeze the same day. Spread on a sheet pan to freeze individually.

seasons.n: Fruit of the Opuntia cactus native to Southern California deserts. Deep magenta flesh is sweet with melon-like flavor.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Peel carefully with gloves to avoid tiny glochid spines.

seasons.n: Tart berry grown in cooler Northern California microclimates. Best when slightly underripe for pies and preserves.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Freeze on a sheet pan then transfer to bags.

seasons.n: Smaller and more aromatic than Persian limes. Most US supply comes from Mexico. Also grown in Florida.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Juice and freeze in ice cube trays for year-round use.

seasons.n: Small tart berries in red black and white varieties. Grown in cooler Northern California areas.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Strip berries from stems with a fork before freezing.

seasons.n: Also called feijoa. Small egg-shaped fruit with tropical flavor. Grows as an ornamental hedge in Southern California.
seasons.st: Ripen at room temperature until slightly soft and fragrant. Refrigerate ripe fruit for a few days.

seasons.n: Sweeter and less acidic than green kiwi. Zespri SunGold from New Zealand dominates the US market.
seasons.st: Refrigerate firm fruit for up to 4 weeks. Ripen at room temperature next to a banana.

seasons.n: Variegated Eureka lemon with pink flesh and striped rind. Grown in coastal Ventura County. Milder than regular lemons.
seasons.st: Store at room temperature for a week or refrigerate for up to a month.

seasons.n: Hardy perennial grown year-round in the Salinas Valley. Hollow stems have mild onion flavor.
seasons.st: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate for up to a week. Snip with scissors -- don't chop.

seasons.n: Sturdy anise-flavored basil essential to Thai and Vietnamese cooking. More heat-tolerant than Italian basil.
seasons.st: Store stems in a glass of water at room temperature. Refrigeration blackens leaves quickly.

seasons.n: French tarragon has the best flavor -- propagated by cuttings only. Anise-scented leaves are classic with chicken and eggs.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Freeze in olive oil for longer storage.

seasons.n: Tropical grass grown in the Central Valley warm season. Use the tender white base for cooking.
seasons.st: Refrigerate whole stalks for 2-3 weeks. Freeze trimmed stalks for several months.

seasons.n: Commercially farmed in California for essential oil and fresh market. Vigorous spreader.
seasons.st: Refrigerate stems in a glass of water covered loosely with a bag. Keeps about a week.

seasons.n: Milder than peppermint with a sweet cool flavor. Classic for mojitos and tabbouleh.
seasons.st: Same as peppermint -- stems in water in the fridge. Dries well for tea.

seasons.n: California bay (Umbellularia) is stronger than Mediterranean bay. Grows wild along the Northern California coast.
seasons.st: Dry leaves flat between paper towels for a week. Store dried in an airtight jar for up to a year.

seasons.n: Japanese herb in green and purple varieties. Increasingly popular at California farmers markets.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate for up to a week. Bruises easily -- handle gently.

seasons.n: Delicate French herb that bolts in summer heat. Grows best in Salinas Valley cool seasons spring and fall.
seasons.st: Very perishable. Refrigerate in a damp paper towel and use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Sweeter milder cousin of oregano. Mediterranean native that thrives in coastal California.
seasons.st: Refrigerate wrapped in damp paper towel for up to a week. Dry or freeze for longer storage.

seasons.n: Premier wild mushroom foraged in coastal Mendocino and Marin oak forests after fall rains. Golden color with apricot aroma.
seasons.st: Brush clean -- don't wash. Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to a week. Saute in butter.

seasons.n: Spring wild mushroom found in Sierra foothills especially in areas of recent wildfire. Honeycomb texture and nutty flavor.
seasons.st: Store in a paper bag in the refrigerator for up to a week. Never eat raw -- always cook.

seasons.n: Cultivated year-round at farms like Far West Fungi in Moss Landing. White shaggy appearance with lobster-like flavor.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to a week. Tear into pieces and sear in a hot pan.

seasons.n: Also called hen of the woods. Cultivated and occasionally wild-foraged in Northern California in fall.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to a week. Tear into clusters and roast at high heat.

seasons.n: Cultivated year-round. Thick meaty stems are the prize -- slice into scallop-sized rounds for searing.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 10 days. The firmest cultivated mushroom.

seasons.n: Cultivated year-round in controlled environments. Long thin white stems with tiny caps. Mild and crunchy.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in original packaging for up to a week. Trim root cluster before using.

seasons.n: Rare aromatic wild mushroom foraged under tanoak and pine in Northern California. Prized in Japanese cuisine.
seasons.st: Wipe clean with a damp cloth. Refrigerate wrapped in paper for 3-5 days. Slice thin and grill.

seasons.n: Wild mushroom foraged in Sierra conifer forests. Thick stem and brown cap with intense umami flavor.
seasons.st: Clean and slice then dry in a dehydrator for long-term storage. Fresh porcini keep 3-5 days refrigerated.

seasons.n: Cultivated year-round in brown and white varieties. Mild nutty flavor. Always cook -- bitter when raw.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in original packaging for up to 10 days. Saute or add to soups.

seasons.n: Wild winter mushroom found in Northern California oak and mixed forests. Thin and fragile with rich smoky flavor.
seasons.st: Brush off debris gently. Refrigerate in a paper bag for 4-5 days. Dries exceptionally well.

seasons.n: California produces 99% of US pistachios concentrated in Kern Tulare and Fresno counties. Trees need desert-like heat.
seasons.st: Store in an airtight container. Refrigerate for 3 months or freeze for up to a year.

seasons.n: Grown in Georgia and the Southeast US. Trees take 6-10 years to produce. Fall harvest after nuts drop naturally.
seasons.st: Store in-shell at room temperature for a few months. Shelled pecans keep longest frozen.

seasons.n: Oregon produces 99% of US hazelnuts in the Willamette Valley. Harvested after falling to the ground.
seasons.st: Store in-shell in a cool dry place for months. Shelled hazelnuts keep best frozen.

seasons.n: Small but growing California crop in Sierra foothills. Must be cooked -- roast score and bake at 425F.
seasons.st: Refrigerate immediately -- chestnuts are perishable unlike other nuts. Use within 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Harvested from pinyon pines in the Sierra Nevada and Great Basin. Labor-intensive hand harvest keeps prices high.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to 3 months or freeze for up to 9 months. High oil content makes them go rancid quickly.

seasons.n: Hawaii's signature nut crop on the Big Island. Hard shell requires specialized cracking equipment.
seasons.st: Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 6 months. Freeze for longer storage.

seasons.n: Cross between broccoli and Chinese kale developed in 1993. Salinas Valley produces year-round supply with cool-season peak.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a produce bag for up to a week. Stems are fully edible.

seasons.n: Aquatic green grown year-round in Central Coast spring-fed beds. Peppery flavor intensifies in warmer months.
seasons.st: Store stems in a glass of water in the refrigerator. Use within 3-4 days.

seasons.n: Belgian endive is forced in darkness to keep it pale and mildly bitter. Salinas Valley is a major US producer.
seasons.st: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate for up to 2 weeks. Keep away from light.

seasons.n: Cool-season green that bolts in summer heat. Seeds can be sown in late February for spring harvest.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a produce bag for up to 5 days. Blanch and freeze for longer storage.

seasons.n: Cultivated dandelion is less bitter than wild. Popular in Italian and Southern cooking. Spring greens are most tender.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a produce bag for up to 5 days. Soak in cold water to reduce bitterness.

seasons.n: Curly pale chicory green with a mild bitter edge. Heart is blanched white and most tender.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a produce bag for up to a week. Wash just before using.

seasons.n: Tender tendrils and leaves of the pea plant. Delicate sweet flavor popular in spring salads and stir-fries.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a produce bag for 2-3 days. Very perishable -- use quickly.

seasons.n: Asian brassica with dark spoon-shaped leaves. Cold tolerant -- flavor improves after light frost.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a produce bag for up to a week. Use in stir-fries or raw in salads.

seasons.n: Winter squash with unique stringy flesh that separates into noodle-like strands. Grown in the Central Valley.
seasons.st: Store whole in a cool dry place for up to 3 months. Cut squash should be refrigerated.

seasons.n: Summer squash including crookneck and straightneck varieties. Prolific Central Valley crop.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Best when small and tender -- large ones become seedy.

seasons.n: Flying-saucer shaped summer squash. Harvest small at 2-3 inches for best flavor and texture.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Scalloped edges and shape make them ideal for stuffing.

seasons.n: Crisp refreshing root vegetable imported year-round from Mexico. Sweet and starchy with apple-like crunch.
seasons.st: Store whole at room temperature for 2-3 weeks. Cut jicama should be refrigerated in water.

seasons.n: Paddle-shaped pads of the prickly pear cactus. Staple in Mexican cuisine with green bean-like flavor.
seasons.st: Refrigerate cleaned pads in a produce bag for up to a week. Remove spines with a vegetable peeler.

seasons.n: Year-round brassica from the Salinas Valley. Sweeter and denser than green cabbage.
seasons.st: Refrigerate whole head for up to 2 weeks. Cut cabbage keeps about a week wrapped tightly.

seasons.n: Smaller and bumpier than slicing cucumbers. Kirby is the most common variety. Harvest at 2-4 inches for best crunch.
seasons.st: Refrigerate for up to a week. Process into pickles within 24 hours of harvest for best texture.

seasons.n: Smaller lateral buds harvested from the same plant after the main terminal bud. Grown primarily around Castroville in Monterey County. More tender than full-size globes with less developed choke, often edible whole. Spring crop is the primary harvest.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a plastic bag for up to 5-7 days. Sprinkle with water to maintain freshness.

seasons.n: Smaller fall harvest of baby artichokes from the Castroville area. Plants are cut back in summer to force dormancy, then regrown for fall production. Fall buds tend to be slightly more compact.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a plastic bag for up to 5-7 days. Sprinkle with water to maintain freshness.

seasons.n: Tender tops harvested from young beet plants, available as a byproduct of beet thinning or from dedicated greens production. Best flavor in cool weather -- bolts and turns tough in summer heat. Rich in iron, vitamin K, and oxalic acid similar to chard.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate in a plastic bag. Use within 3-5 days as leaves wilt quickly.

seasons.n: Fresh shelling blackeye peas from the Central Valley. California Blackeye No. 5 and No. 46 are the dominant commercial varieties. Heat-loving legume planted after soil reaches 65F, harvested when pods are plump and easily snapped. Fresh peas cook much faster than dried.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unshelled in a plastic bag for up to 5 days. Shelled peas should be used within 2-3 days or frozen.

seasons.n: Georgia, Arkansas, and Tennessee are leading producers of fresh shelling cowpeas. Southern states have longer warm seasons that extend availability beyond the California window.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unshelled in a plastic bag for up to 5 days. Shelled peas should be used within 2-3 days or frozen.

seasons.n: Short-day plant that only flowers when daylength drops below 12 hours. In Southern California, blossoms appear in September and fruit ripens in October. Produces prolifically until first frost. Mild, crisp flesh similar to a cross between cucumber and jicama.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwrapped in the crisper drawer for up to 3-4 weeks. Do not peel until ready to use.

seasons.n: Mexico supplies roughly 89% of US chayote imports. Available in most Latin grocery stores year-round, with peak supply in fall and winter. Also imported from Costa Rica and Guatemala to fill supply gaps.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwrapped in the crisper drawer for up to 3-4 weeks. Do not peel until ready to use.

seasons.n: Also called gai lan or kai lan. Cool-season brassica that bolts quickly in heat above 75F. Thick, glossy stems are the prized part -- harvest at 6-8 inches before white flower buds open. Fast crop at 55-70 days from seed. Flavor is sweeter and less bitter than conventional broccoli.
seasons.st: Wrap stems in damp paper towel and refrigerate in a plastic bag. Use within 4-5 days. Stems stay crisp longer than leaves.

seasons.n: Broad-leaved endive grown commercially in the Salinas Valley. Best planted in summer for fall/winter harvest -- bolts before reaching full size if spring-planted. Outer leaves are pleasantly bitter, inner heart is mild and buttery. Takes 85-100 days to mature. Excellent braised or in Italian wedding soup.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate for up to 5-7 days. Inner leaves keep longer than outer ones.

seasons.n: Florida supplies the East Coast and supplements California production during winter months. Same growing requirements as California -- cool-season crop that needs consistent moisture.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate for up to 5-7 days. Inner leaves keep longer than outer ones.

seasons.n: Heirloom variety (often Burpee's Golden or Touchstone Gold) that does not bleed like red beets. Sweeter and milder than red varieties. Matures in 55-65 days, best in cool weather between 50-70F. Flesh turns rich golden-orange when roasted. Prized at farmers markets for salads and roasting.
seasons.st: Remove greens immediately (they draw moisture from the root). Refrigerate unwashed roots in a plastic bag for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Unripe tomatoes harvested firm before coloring, or heirloom varieties like Green Zebra and Cherokee Green that stay green when ripe. Most common at farmers markets in September-October as growers clear plants. Tart, firm flesh is ideal for frying, pickling, and chutney.
seasons.st: Store at room temperature out of direct sunlight. Will begin to ripen within a week. Refrigerate only if fully green and firm -- keeps 1-2 weeks.

seasons.n: Crinkled, deeply veined leaves are more tender and delicate than smooth-leaf cabbage. Best in cool weather. Takes 70-110 days depending on variety. Lighter and more pliable than green cabbage, making it superior for stuffed cabbage rolls.
seasons.st: Refrigerate whole head in a plastic bag for up to 1-2 weeks. Cut cabbage dries out faster -- wrap tightly in plastic.

seasons.n: Santa Maria Valley cool-season production. Quality and sweetness are best after light frost exposure in winter months.
seasons.st: Refrigerate whole head in a plastic bag for up to 1-2 weeks. Cut cabbage dries out faster -- wrap tightly in plastic.

seasons.n: Imperial Sweets are short-day varieties with high sugar-to-pyruvic acid ratio, resulting in mild sweetness without the bite. Planted in fall, harvested starting late April. The Imperial Valley is one of the largest sweet onion districts in the US.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated area. Sweet onions have higher water content and spoil faster than storage onions -- use within 2-3 weeks. Refrigerate for longer storage.

seasons.n: San Joaquin Valley harvest begins around Bakersfield in late May and moves north through Kern and Fresno counties. Intermediate-day sweet varieties extend availability after the Imperial Valley deal finishes.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated area. Sweet onions have higher water content and spoil faster than storage onions -- use within 2-3 weeks. Refrigerate for longer storage.

seasons.n: Short-day white varieties planted in fall and harvested spring. Sharp, clean flavor when raw that mellows when cooked. Essential in Mexican cuisine for salsas and pickled onions.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, dark location with good air circulation for 2-4 weeks. White onions have thinner, more papery skin and don't store as long as yellow varieties.

seasons.n: Intermediate-day white varieties from the San Joaquin Valley extend the California season after Imperial Valley finishes. Harvest progresses from Bakersfield north through the valley.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, dark location with good air circulation for 2-4 weeks. White onions have thinner, more papery skin and don't store as long as yellow varieties.

seasons.n: Mexican white onions fill the gap when California production ends. Mexico is the primary source for US white onion imports, with Sinaloa and Chihuahua as major growing regions.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, dark location with good air circulation for 2-4 weeks. White onions have thinner, more papery skin and don't store as long as yellow varieties.

seasons.n: Cool-season crop grown commercially in the Central Valley during fall and winter. Greens are ready to pick within a month of sowing. Best flavor at 50-65F; high temperatures cause bitterness. Traditional Southern cooking green, excellent braised with vinegar and smoked pork.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate in a plastic bag. Use within 3-5 days. Blanch and freeze for longer storage.

seasons.n: Southeast US states (Georgia, Alabama, Tennessee) are major producers of turnip greens. Longer mild-winter season extends availability.
seasons.st: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate in a plastic bag. Use within 3-5 days. Blanch and freeze for longer storage.

seasons.n: Tiny bulb onion (under 1 inch diameter) harvested young from dense plantings. White, red, and gold varieties available. Mild, sweet flavor prized for braising, pickling, and stews.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated spot for 2-4 weeks. Refrigerate in a mesh bag for up to 1 month. Avoid plastic bags which trap moisture.

seasons.n: Early-season desert production from the Imperial Valley onion region. Short-day varieties planted in fall for spring harvest.
seasons.st: Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated spot for 2-4 weeks. Refrigerate in a mesh bag for up to 1 month.

seasons.n: Chinese heirloom daikon-type radish with pale green skin and striking pink-to-magenta interior. Takes 55-65 days to mature. Flavor sweetens as temperatures drop in fall and winter. Best planted midsummer for late fall harvest. Sizes up to 2-4 inches in diameter for optimal quality.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a plastic bag with a damp paper towel for 2-3 weeks. Remove greens promptly as they draw moisture from the root.

seasons.n: Limited California production on small-scale farms in warm inland areas. Requires 7-12 months of hot weather and soil temperatures above 70F. Primarily dasheen and eddoe types. Harvested in fall when lower leaves yellow.
seasons.st: Store unwashed in a cool, dark, dry place for up to 1 week. Refrigerate loosely wrapped for up to 2 weeks. Do not eat raw; taro must be cooked thoroughly to neutralize calcium oxalate crystals.

seasons.n: Year-round tropical imports from the Philippines, Fiji, Samoa, and other Pacific Island nations. The US imports over 64,000 metric tons annually. Also imported from Mexico, Costa Rica, and Ecuador.
seasons.st: Store unwashed in a cool, dark, dry place for up to 1 week. Refrigerate loosely wrapped for up to 2 weeks. Must be cooked before eating.

seasons.n: Broad chicory family includes radicchio, Belgian endive, frisee, and puntarelle. Salinas Valley is the primary California production region, with radicchio harvested May through January. Cool-season crop thriving at 45-75F. Takes 85-100 days to mature. Coastal fog belt is ideal.
seasons.st: Wrap loosely in damp paper towels and refrigerate in a plastic bag for 1-2 weeks. Radicchio heads keep longest; loose-leaf types like frisee keep 5-7 days.

seasons.n: Winter Central Valley production fills the gap when Salinas is too cold for field crops. Radicchio rosa season typically begins in January. Shorter window due to warming spring temperatures which cause bolting.
seasons.st: Wrap loosely in damp paper towels and refrigerate for 1-2 weeks.

seasons.n: Japanese mustard green with feathery, deeply serrated leaves and mild peppery flavor. Fast-growing at 40 days to maturity. More bolt-resistant than most brassica greens. Grown commercially alongside other salad mix components in the Salinas Valley.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a loosely sealed bag or container lined with paper towels for 5-7 days. Wilts quickly at room temperature.

seasons.n: Coastal fog belt allows extended cool-season production. Often included in spring mix and mesclun blends. Mild marine climate reduces bolting pressure during shoulder months.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a loosely sealed container lined with paper towels for 5-7 days.

seasons.n: Also called yu choy sum, meaning 'oil vegetable' in Cantonese. Produces fleshy pale green stalks with broad dark green leaves and small yellow flowers. Grown extensively by Southeast Asian farmers in Fresno County. Fast-growing at 30-45 days. Bolts quickly in summer heat. Milder and sweeter than bok choy.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed, wrapped in a damp paper towel inside a plastic bag for 3-5 days. Stems should be firm and crisp; yellowing flowers indicate age.

seasons.n: Coastal production extends the window slightly into early summer thanks to cooler temperatures. Grown alongside other Asian brassicas for specialty and wholesale markets.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a damp paper towel inside a plastic bag for 3-5 days.

seasons.n: Semi-aquatic tropical green also called kangkong or ong choy. Federally classified as a noxious weed; California requires CDFA cultivation permits. The Ching Quat variety is preferred in California as it grows in damp soil without free water. Thrives at 68-86F. Extremely perishable.
seasons.st: Highly perishable. Refrigerate unwashed with stems in water or wrapped in damp paper towels for 1-3 days maximum. Use as soon as possible after purchase.

seasons.n: Year-round imports from Southeast Asian countries supplement domestic California supply, especially during winter months when local production ceases. Available at Asian grocery stores and specialty distributors.
seasons.st: Extremely perishable. Refrigerate and use within 1-2 days of purchase.

seasons.n: Italian heirloom beet with distinctive concentric pink and white ring pattern when sliced. Also called candy cane or bull's-eye beet. Takes 50-60 days to harvest. Flavor declines and rings fade with cooking heat. Best eaten raw or lightly roasted.
seasons.st: Remove greens (store separately for 2-3 days). Refrigerate unwashed roots in a plastic bag for 2-3 weeks. Do not peel until ready to use.

seasons.n: Spring and fall production in the Central Valley avoids summer heat stress which causes bitterness and poor ring development.
seasons.st: Remove greens and refrigerate unwashed roots in a plastic bag for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Thistle-like relative of the artichoke grown for its thick, celery-like leaf stalks. Requires blanching (wrapping stalks in burlap for 4-5 weeks) to reduce bitterness. Takes 120-150 days to mature. Thrives in mild, damp coastal California. Popular in Italian and North African cuisine.
seasons.st: Wrap blanched stalks in damp paper towels and refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 1 week. Trim and peel tough outer fibers before cooking. Place cut pieces in acidulated water to prevent browning.

seasons.n: Succulent summer annual with thick, fleshy leaves and a mild lemony, slightly peppery flavor. Both wild-foraged and commercially cultivated. Rich in omega-3 fatty acids, more than any other leafy green. Common agricultural weed rebranded as a gourmet green.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a loosely sealed container lined with a damp paper towel for 3-5 days. Wilts and yellows quickly at room temperature.

seasons.n: Coastal production season is slightly shorter due to cooler marine influence. Found at farmers markets and in specialty salad mixes.
seasons.st: Refrigerate in a loosely sealed container for 3-5 days.

seasons.n: Perennial herb with bright, tart, lemony flavor from oxalic acid content. Garden sorrel and French sorrel are the most common culinary varieties. Hardy perennial that regrows vigorously each spring. Flavor is sharpest in cool weather; leaves become tough and overly acidic in summer heat. Classic in French cuisine for sauces and soups.
seasons.st: Refrigerate unwashed in a plastic bag lined with paper towels for 3-5 days. Wilts quickly once picked. Freezes well when blanched for use in cooked dishes.

seasons.n: Also called corn salad or lamb's lettuce. Delicate, nutty, mildly sweet rosette green prized in French cuisine. Thrives in cool temperatures (45-65F) and tolerates light frost. Takes 40-50 days to mature. One of the few fresh salad greens available through winter.
seasons.st: Very delicate. Refrigerate unwashed in a loosely sealed container lined with a damp paper towel for 3-5 days maximum. Handle gently as leaves bruise easily.

seasons.n: World's largest tree fruit, imported primarily from India, Thailand, Malaysia, and the Philippines. Also sourced from Mexico and Guatemala. Flesh is sweet and aromatic with a distinctive tropical flavor. Available as whole fruit, pre-cut fresh, frozen, and canned. Unripe jackfruit is used as a meat substitute.
seasons.st: Whole uncut jackfruit keeps at room temperature for 3-5 days or refrigerated up to 1 week. Cut flesh should be refrigerated in an airtight container and used within 5 days. Freezes well for up to 2 months.

seasons.n: Also called Chinese date. Popular California varieties include Li (large, sweet, crisp), Lang (ideal for drying, slightly tangy), and GA866 (UC Davis variety, high sugar, apple-like flavor). Thrives in hot Central Valley summers. Fruit ripens unevenly, transitioning from green to mahogany brown.
seasons.st: Fresh jujubes keep at room temperature for about 1 week. Refrigerate in a perforated bag for 2-3 weeks. Dried jujubes store indefinitely at room temperature.

seasons.n: Grows well in Northern California's hot interior valleys. Drought-resistant once established. Trees need full sun and hot summers for ample fruit set.
seasons.st: Fresh jujubes keep at room temperature for about 1 week. Refrigerate in a perforated bag for 2-3 weeks.

seasons.n: Also called carambola. Small-scale production in frost-free coastal Southern California. Fruit has a distinctive five-pointed star cross-section. Sweet varieties (B-10, Arkin) preferred for fresh eating. Trees bloom in spring, summer, and fall, enabling multiple fruiting periods.
seasons.st: Firm green star fruit ripens at room temperature in 2-4 days. Ripe yellow fruit refrigerates well for 1-2 weeks. Cut slices should be used within 2-3 days.

seasons.n: Imported year-round from Taiwan, Thailand, and Mexico. Florida is the main US domestic producer. About 59% of US availability comes from imports. Sweet and tart varieties available; sweet types dominate the US market.
seasons.st: Imported fruit keeps 1-2 weeks refrigerated. Select firm, bright yellow fruit with minimal brown edges.

seasons.n: Grown commercially in the Imperial Valley, where the dry climate, intense sunlight, and irrigation support production. Takes about 14 months from planting to harvest. Fresh stalks sold at farmers markets and grocery stores for chewing and juicing.
seasons.st: Fresh whole stalks keep at room temperature for 1-2 weeks. Refrigerate cut pieces wrapped in plastic for up to 2 weeks. Sugarcane juice oxidizes quickly and should be consumed within hours of pressing.

seasons.n: Fresh sugarcane stalks available year-round in US grocery stores, largely sourced from tropical producing regions including Central America and the Caribbean.
seasons.st: Store whole stalks upright in a cool place for up to 2 weeks. Refrigerate cut pieces in plastic wrap for 1-2 weeks.

seasons.n: India is the world's largest tamarind producer. Pods contain a sweet-sour pulp used in cooking, beverages, and candy. Both sweet and sour varieties available. Dried pods and paste are the most common forms in US markets.
seasons.st: Dried tamarind pods keep at room temperature in an airtight container for up to 1 year. Tamarind paste refrigerates well for several months. Fresh pods last 2-3 weeks refrigerated.

seasons.n: Thailand is a major exporter of sweet tamarind, with peak harvest from late November through March. Thai sweet tamarind is particularly prized for fresh eating.
seasons.st: Fresh sweet tamarind pods keep 2-3 weeks at room temperature or up to 1 month refrigerated.

seasons.n: Mexican tamarind is widely used for agua fresca, candy, and sauces. Pods are available fresh in spring and summer at Latin American grocery stores throughout California.
seasons.st: Fresh pods keep 2-3 weeks at room temperature. Processed tamarind candy and paste have a long shelf life.

seasons.n: Accidentally created in 1883 in Santa Cruz, California by horticulturist James Harvey Logan. A cross between blackberry and raspberry with dark red, elongated fruit. Distinctive tart-sweet flavor. Very perishable and rarely found in mainstream retail.
seasons.st: Extremely perishable. Refrigerate immediately unwashed in a single layer on a paper towel-lined container. Use within 2-3 days. Freezes well for up to 6 months.

seasons.n: Grows well in coastal California berry-producing regions with moderate temperatures. Most popular in the Pacific Northwest but has historical roots in California.
seasons.st: Handle gently and refrigerate immediately. Best eaten within 2-3 days. Excellent for jams, pies, and freezing.

seasons.n: A raspberry-blackberry cross developed in Scotland in 1979 and named after the River Tay. Larger and sweeter than loganberries with a dark purple color when fully ripe. Very limited commercial availability; mostly found at u-pick farms and specialty markets.
seasons.st: Very perishable. Refrigerate unwashed in a shallow container with paper towel lining. Use within 2-3 days. Freezes well for up to 6 months.

seasons.n: Suited to California's coastal berry-growing regions where cool marine influence prevents heat stress. Vigorous grower with complex raspberry-blackberry flavor.
seasons.st: Handle gently and refrigerate immediately in a single layer. Consume within 2-3 days. Excellent for preserves, syrups, and freezing.

seasons.n: Trademarked name for the Jamaican tangelo, a natural hybrid of grapefruit, orange, and tangerine discovered in Jamaica. Rough, loose-fitting greenish-yellow skin but exceptionally juicy, sweet-tart flesh. Available at specialty grocers and some supermarkets during winter months.
seasons.st: Keeps at room temperature for up to 5 days. Refrigerate for 2-3 weeks. Peels easily by hand like a tangerine.

seasons.n: Physalis peruviana, also known as goldenberry. Small golden-orange fruit enclosed in a papery husk. Grown on small scale in frost-free areas of Southern California. Sweet-tart flavor with tropical notes.
seasons.st: Keep in husks until ready to eat. Refrigerate in a single layer for 2-3 weeks (husks protect the fruit). Remove husks and rinse before eating.

seasons.n: Colombia is the world's leading exporter of cape gooseberries. Approved for US import since 2014. Available year-round in US supermarkets as part of berry programs.
seasons.st: Imported berries keep 2-3 weeks refrigerated in their clamshell packaging. Leave husks on until ready to eat for maximum shelf life.

seasons.n: Same species as cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana), marketed as goldenberry in the United States. Small golden fruit in a papery husk with sweet-tart tropical flavor. Limited small-scale local production in frost-free Southern California.
seasons.st: Keep husked until ready to eat. Refrigerate for 2-3 weeks. Remove husk and rinse before eating.

seasons.n: Goldenberry is the primary US marketing name for Colombian cape gooseberries. Colombia dominates global exports. Available year-round in US supermarkets, often in the berry section.
seasons.st: Store in original clamshell packaging, refrigerated, for 2-3 weeks. Leave papery husks intact for longest shelf life.
